questions.question: Why is an append-only ledger usually preferred over mutating a balance row directly?
options: Because reconstructable history and explicit corrections matter more than write simplicity.
Because append-only storage guarantees zero latency for balance reads.
Because mutable balances cannot be replicated across regions.
Because a ledger removes the need for idempotency keys.
questions.explanation: Financial systems need trustworthy history, not just the latest value.

questions.question: What is the main value of idempotency in a payment API?
options: It lets the same retry complete safely without creating duplicate money movement.
It compresses ledger entries before archiving them.
It removes the need for reconciliation with external processors.
It guarantees that downstream banks settle instantly.
questions.explanation: Retries are expected in payments, so duplicate side effects must be blocked.

questions.question: Which invariant most directly protects ledger correctness?
options: Each committed posting batch is balanced before it is accepted.
Each customer has at most one balance row.
Each API response is cached at the edge.
Each reconciliation job runs in under one second.
questions.explanation: A balanced batch is the accounting invariant that keeps books sane.

questions.question: Why should corrections usually be modeled as reversals instead of updates in place?
options: Reversals preserve audit history and make investigation possible after mistakes.
Updates in place cannot be replicated by databases.
Reversals are always cheaper than updates.
Updates in place break all balance materialization code.
questions.explanation: Financial systems need a durable trail of what changed and why.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with using balance projections or snapshots?
options: They speed reads but require rebuild and drift-detection mechanisms.
They make reconciliation unnecessary once enabled.
They force total ordering across all accounts.
They remove the need for retention policies.
questions.explanation: Serving speed improves, but operational complexity moves into projection management.

questions.question: Which signal best reveals settlement drift between the internal ledger and a processor?
options: Discrepancy count and unreconciled amount by source and aging bucket.
Median HTTP header size on payment requests.
Number of dashboard viewers from finance.
Ratio of GET requests to POST requests.
questions.explanation: You want a direct view of missing or mismatched money movement.

questions.question: What is the most credible answer if payment status must be fast but books must remain exact?
options: Separate customer-facing status projections from the ledger source of truth.
Store status only in the processor and skip internal postings.
Use one global lock so every read is perfectly current.
Treat every pending payment as settled for simplicity.
questions.explanation: A serving layer can move fast while the ledger remains authoritative.

questions.question: What changes most when retry volume spikes during a processor outage?
options: Idempotency storage and duplicate-suppression correctness become part of core capacity planning.
Double-entry accounting stops mattering until recovery is complete.
Balance snapshots should be disabled for all customers.
Audit logs can be dropped temporarily to save storage.
questions.explanation: Incident-time retry amplification is a design input, not an afterthought.
